content: {"personas": [{"perspective": "Scientific", "name": "Dr. Hiroshi Tanaka", "expertise": "Clinical endocrinologist at Kyoto University Graduate School of Medicine, specializing in randomized trial design and GRADE evidence synthesis for dietary interventions in metabolic syndrome. Has first-authored three PRISMA-compliant meta-analyses of fasting and time-restricted eating protocols, and serves on the Cochrane Metabolic and Endocrine Disorders editorial board. His laboratory also runs human glucose-clamp studies that measure insulin sensitivity directly.", "question_focus": "He would prioritize the hierarchy of evidence: which endpoints (FBS, HbA1c, HOMA-IR, LDL-C, IL-6) are supported by GRADE-high evidence across independent RCT meta-analyses, what the point estimates and 95% confidence intervals are, and how the heterogeneity (I² up to 93.8%) is explained by protocol, population, and duration moderators. He would look specifically at effect-size consistency between the 10-RCT 2025 MetS review, the 15-RCT 2025 cardiometabolic review, the 8-RCT MetS GRADE review, and the Lancet eClinicalMedicine umbrella review."}, {"perspective": "Regulatory", "name": "Maria Escalante, JD", "expertise": "Food law attorney and former legal counsel at the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) Nutrition Panel, now in private practice advising both member-state health ministries and dietary-intervention sponsors on Article 13 and Article 14 health-claim substantiation under EC 1924/2006. Also holds a master's in public health law and has filed FDA Citizen Petitions concerning dietary guidance statements on food packaging.", "question_focus": "She would focus on whether intermittent fasting has been subjected to any formal regulatory opinion by FDA, EMA, EFSA, or Health Canada as a therapeutic intervention for metabolic disease; whether commercial IF apps and programs make claims that cross into unapproved therapeutic territory; the FDA draft guidance on Dietary Guidance Statements and how it applies to fasting; and how conference-abstract-only evidence (the 2024 AHA press release without peer review) interacts with fair-marketing and enforcement authority."}, {"perspective": "Industry", "name": "Rajiv Menon, MBA", "expertise": "Chief product officer at a Series C digital health company operating a fasting-tracking subscription app with 4.8 million monthly active users across iOS and Android. Previously led growth analytics at a GLP-1 telehealth prescribing platform. He has seen real-world adherence, retention cohorts, A/B test data on protocol types, and the downstream health outcomes users self-report, and he has managed CE-mark and FDA 510(k) strategy for digital therapeutic companion products.", "question_focus": "He would focus on the market reality the academic literature misses: day-2 and day-30 adherence curves, which fasting protocol (16:8 TRE, ADF, 5:2) users actually sustain, what consumer wellness integrations drive retention, what the subscription economics tell us about willingness-to-pay for disease prevention, and how the 2024 CV-mortality story affected user churn and acquisition. He would contrast what RCT participants do (supervised, paid) with what real subscribers do (unsupervised, self-paying)."}, {"perspective": "Economic", "name": "Dr. Eleanor Whitfield", "expertise": "Senior health economist at the London School of Economics Personal Social Services Research Unit, specializing in decision-analytic cost-effectiveness modeling of lifestyle interventions for type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome prevention. Builds Markov and microsimulation models of QALY and cost-of-illness trajectories using NICE reference case methods, and has published budget-impact analyses of lifestyle programs versus semaglutide for the NHS.", "question_focus": "She would focus on the cost-effectiveness gap between IF and the standard of care: ICER per QALY gained, 10-year budget impact versus no-intervention and versus GLP-1 agonists, the economic value of modest but durable HbA1c reductions across a population at risk, and the real-world economics of free vs subscription IF tools. She would flag the near-absence of IF-specific cost-effectiveness data as a decision-making obstacle for payers."}, {"perspective": "Public_Health", "name": "Dr. Amara Okonkwo, MPH", "expertise": "Population epidemiologist at the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention, focused on the epidemic transition from undernutrition to metabolic syndrome across Sub-Saharan African and Middle Eastern populations. Designs and leads community-based prospective cohorts using case-control and self-reported dietary pattern methods, and advises on national dietary guidelines for countries with large Ramadan-observing populations where dawn-to-sunset fasting is a natural real-world IF model.", "question_focus": "She would focus on population-scale, free-living evidence: the Ramadan systematic-review literature, the 2024 US NHANES-based 91% CV-mortality signal for <8-hour eating windows and its methodological limitations (two-day recall, reverse causation, unmeasured confounding), and the generalizability of predominantly-Western RCT findings to LMIC populations with different cardiometabolic baselines, food environments, and healthcare-access profiles."}]}
